text: When to suspect drug allergy (NICE CG183, 1.1 Assessment): The reaction is more likely to be caused by drug allergy if it occurred during or after use of the drug and: the drug is known to cause that type of reaction, or the person has previously had a similar reaction.

Recording drug allergy status (NICE CG183, 1.2 Documentation): Document people's drug allergy status in their medical records using 1 of the following: 'drug allergy', 'none known', 'unable to ascertain'.

Minimum information to record for a drug allergy (NICE CG183, 1.2 Documentation): Record all of the following at a minimum: the drug name, the signs, symptoms and severity of the reaction, the date when the reaction occurred.
Non-selective NSAID avoidance (NICE CG183, 1.4 Management): People with a suspected allergy to a non-selective NSAID need to avoid all non-selective NSAIDs, including over-the-counter preparations.

NSAID referral criteria (NICE CG183, 1.4 Management): Refer people who need treatment with an NSAID to a specialist drug allergy service if they have had a suspected allergic reaction with symptoms such as anaphylaxis, severe angioedema or an asthmatic reaction.

Beta-lactam antibiotic referral (NICE CG183, 1.4 Management): Refer people with suspected beta-lactam allergy if they need treatment for a disease or condition that can only be treated by a beta-lactam antibiotic or are likely to need beta-lactam antibiotics frequently.

Multiple-class antibiotic allergy referral (NICE CG183, 1.4 Management): Consider referring people to a specialist drug allergy service if they are not able to take beta-lactam antibiotics and at least 1 other class of antibiotic because of suspected allergy.

Drug allergy information to carry (NICE CG183, 1.3 Providing information): Advise people to carry information they are given about their drug allergy at all times and to share this whenever they visit a healthcare professional.
